coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based nanomaterials for fluorescent sensing of toxic elements in environmental samples: Strategies for enhanced performance
coredata.dc:creator: González-González, Reyna Berenice
Morales-Murillo, Martha Beatriz
Martínez-Prado, María Adriana
Melchor-Martínez, Elda M.
Ahmed, Ishtiaq
Bilal, Muhammad
Parra-Saldívar, Roberto
Iqbal, Hafiz M.N.
coredata.dc:description: Rapid industrialization and manufacturing expansion have caused heavy metal pollution, which is a critical environmental issue faced by global population. In addition, the disadvantages presented by conventional detection methods such as the requirement of sophisticated instruments and qualified personnel have led to the development of novel nanosensors. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been presented as a multifunctional nanomaterial alternative for the accurate detection of heavy metal ions in water systems. The capacity of CDs to detect contaminants in wastewater –including heavy metals– can be found in the literature; however, to the best of our knowledge, none of them discusses the most recent strategies to enhance their performance. Therefore, in this review, beyond presenting successful examples of the use of CDs for the detection of metal ions, we further discuss the strategies to enhance their photoluminescence properties and their performance for environmental monitoring. In this manner, strategies such as heteroatom-doping and surface passivation are reviewed in detail, as well as describing the mechanisms and the effect of precursors and synthesis methods. Finally, the current challenges are described in detail to propose some recommendations for further research.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid industrialization and manufacturing expansion have caused heavy metal pollution, which is a critical environmental issue faced by global population. In addition, the disadvantages presented by conventional detection methods such as the requirement of sophisticated instruments and qualified personnel have led to the development of novel nanosensors. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been presented as a multifunctional nanomaterial alternative for the accurate detection of heavy metal ions in water systems. The capacity of CDs to detect contaminants in wastewater –including heavy metals– can be found in the literature; however, to the best of our knowledge, none of them discusses the most recent strategies to enhance their performance. Therefore, in this review, beyond presenting successful examples of the use of CDs for the detection of metal ions, we further discuss the strategies to enhance their photoluminescence properties and their performance for environmental monitoring. In this manner, strategies such as heteroatom-doping and surface passivation are reviewed in detail, as well as describing the mechanisms and the effect of precursors and synthesis methods. Finally, the current challenges are described in detail to propose some recommendations for further research.

Nitrogen and Phosphorous doped carbon dots
Nitrogen and Phosphorous doped graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots
Nitrogen, phosphorus and sulfur-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is a major environmental issue faced by the present-day world's population, that can reach alarmingly toxic levels. Rapid industrialization leads to manufacturing expansion which causes a ceaseless surge in metal concentration and critical pollution problems due to inappropriate disposal and discarding of industrialized waste products directly into water bodies and land areas (Dixit et al., 2015). Heavy metals are the primary elements of the earth's surface which exist as enduring environmental contaminants that cannot be exclusively biodegraded but can only be transformed into nontoxic forms. Large quantities of such metals can become toxic to all forms of life including microorganisms, plants, animals, and humans. Moreover, they can also enter the environment through water and soil, where they can bioaccumulate for a long time. Accumulation of toxic metals into the human body creates severe health consequences such as growth and developmental abnormalities, neuromuscular defects, and failure of metabolic activities (Mishra et al., 2019). Wagh et al. (2018) analyzed the Kadava River Basin located in India to evaluate the health risk from the groundwater. The heavy metal concentrations found by the authors represent serious environmental and health concerns.

The significance of water pollution has urged researchers to develop techniques to detect heavy metal contaminants. Numerous techniques have been used for the detection of heavy metal ions which can be divided into three main categories; spectroscopic detection techniques, electrochemical detection techniques, and optical detection techniques (Malik et al., 2019). Some conventional detection techniques face considerable obstacles for proper heavy metal detection. Among their disadvantages are the geographic limitations of certain contaminated locations, high cost, specificity, soil disturbance, and low efficiency, among others (Li et al., 2019). Therefore, research efforts have been directed on the development of innovative sensors for the sensitive detection not only of heavy metal ions (Devaraj et al., 2021) but also for the monitoring of toxic gases (Le et al., 2022) and drugs and biomarkers in the human body (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2021a, 2021b).

Through the last decades, nanotechnology has been developed and its applications have now been extended to almost all fields of science and technology. Different nanoparticles including nanofibers, reversed micelles, nanogels, and organic and inorganic nanoparticles such as gold nanoparticles, quantum dots, and carbon nanotubes have an ample range of applications (Muktha et al., 2020). Nanomaterials have been successfully applied as catalysts (Doan et al., 2021; Nguyen et al., 2022), antibacterial, and nanosensors (Nguyen et al., 2022). Among the large variety of nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials like carbon dots (CDs) have been widely studied for environmental purposes owing to good stabilities, non-toxicity, and abundant sources (González-González et al., 2022a; Lopez-Cantu et al., 2022). Moreover, CDs possess several benefits such as biocompatibility, low-cost synthesis process, chemical stability, and fluorescence (FL) properties (Chu et al., 2019; Vinoth Kumar et al., 2021).

Existing reviews provide a wider perspective of the capacity of CDs to detect contaminants in wastewater (Xiang-jun et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). However, none of them discuss the recent strategies to enhance their performance in the detection of metal ions. Thus, herein carbon dots are presented as metal ion detectors for environmental samples discussing their different mechanisms and the strategies that researchers employ for enhanced performances. Finally, the current challenges are discussed to suggest possible future research for optimal heavy metal detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent advances on CDs for metal sensing applications

ce:section.ce:para: CDs are well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with particle sizes less than 10 nm (Kang et al., 2020). The structure of CDs is critical for the understanding of their main features. It consists of two main parts: a spherical-like core formed by stacking multiple graphene fragments in an ordered or disordered manner, and a surface rich in functional groups (Yan et al., 2019). In the light of their variation in crystal structure and quantum confinement, CDs are typically classified as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). CQDs consist of multiple layers of graphitic sheets, they are spherical crystalline nanoparticles that exhibit quantum confinement; GQDs are single-layer or multi-layer flaky crystalline nanoparticles with conjugated π-electron graphene layers that also exhibit quantum confinement; CNDs are spherical nanoparticles with an amorphous carbon structure that lack quantum confinement (Bin Chen et al., 2019; González-González et al., 2022a; Semeniuk et al., 2019).

In contrast to semiconductor quantum dots, CDs exhibit numerous fascinating merits, including tunable FL, nontoxicity, long-term photobleaching resistance, and dispersibility in water, which facilitate their application in many areas (Bin Chen et al., 2019). Such properties enable their application as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions, such as cobalt (II) ion (Co2+), iron (III) ion (Fe3+), mercury (II) ion (Hg2+), and lead (II) ion (Pb2+), which have a potentially high level of toxicity to living biological organisms (Yarur et al., 2019). For example, Liu et al. (2017a) reported the use of CDs prepared from CA and diethylenetriamine for sensing Co2+; useful for environmental monitoring, medicine safety, and cellular research. Similarly, mercury ions –considered extremely toxic to human health– have been detected by CDs; for example, Wangnbsp et al. (2016) reported an eco-friendly and highly sensitive mercury ionic probe using CDs from cherry tomatoes. On the other hand, Kumar et al. (2017) reported a simple, rapid, and cost-effective synthesis method to obtain fluorescent CDs for the detection of Pb2+ ions using leaves of Ocimum Sanctum as the carbon source.

Different methods have been developed for the preparation of CDs aimed to detect metal ions. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , there are two approaches in the preparation of CDs: the top-down approach in which the nanomaterials are prepared by breaking down either through physical or chemical forces the bulk materials; whereas, the assembling of atoms or molecules until reaching the desired nanomaterial structure is termed as the bottom-up approach (Muktha et al., 2020).

The bottom-up strategy includes methods such as hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, ultrasonic, acid dehydration, and pyrolysis methods. Among them, hydrothermal and microwave-assisted methods are the most widely used for the preparation of CDs (Muktha et al., 2020). Hydrothermal, as a preferable constructive synthesis technique, is extensively approved by researchers since a precise control of the particles’ size can be achieved. This technique consists of the reaction of solid material with an aqueous solution in a reaction vessel at controlled conditions of temperature and pressures using water as a solvent (Abid et al., 2021). Lin et al. (2019a) employed the hydrothermal method to synthesize CDs co-doped with nitrogen and phosphorous using O-phosphorylethanolamine (O-PE) and citric acid (CA), which were applied as a turn-on fluorescent probe for cadmium (II) ion (Cd2+) for its use in health and environmental monitoring.

The microwave-assisted method is considered simple, safe, and affordable along with a fast reaction time (Mansuriya and Altintas, 2021). During the microwave-assisted method, the generation of carbon nanoparticles is achieved under the homogeneous and direct heating effects of microwave irradiation on the precursors (Ng et al., 2021; Omoriyekomwan et al., 2021). This method enables homogeneous heating and enhanced mass transfer of volatiles, thus, being the preferable selection from many researchers to prepare CDs (Xia et al., 2019). As a representative example of the employment of this method, Xu et al. (2018) reported the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs used for the effective fluorometric determination of Hg2+ in spiked tap and lake water samples. On the other hand, CDs can be formed by reducing the size of their precursors or bulk materials. In this respect, different synthesis techniques follow this principle, such as laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical synthesis, plasma treatment, and chemical oxidation. Usually, these methods have been used to synthesize CDs from residual waste materials, particularly from organic wastes.

The electrochemical technique is frequently used by researchers to synthesize CDs since it is a straightforward method. During this technique and using two electrodes, a potential difference is applied in a carbon precursor resulting in the carbonization, thus forming carbon nanoparticles (Goswami et al., 2021). The electrochemical method was used by Fu et al. (2017) to prepare CDs via electrolysis of graphite in sodium hydroxide (NaOH) aqueous solution, which were then applied as a novel sensor of high selectivity for the detection of Aluminum (III) ion (Al3+) in real environmental water samples.

Pyrolysis has been also applied as a top-down approach for the formation of CDs. This method involves high temperatures under inert atmospheres resulting in the decomposition and carbonization of carbon precursors to form CDs (Goswami et al., 2021). In this regard, Tan et al. (2014) prepared CDs via pyrolysis of sago waste as a potential optical probe for the detection of several metal ions including copper (Cu) and Pb (II) ions, demonstrating that agricultural waste can be used as a precursor for high-value optical sensors. Similarly, nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared through direct pyrolysis and they were successfully employed for the selective detection of Fe3+ in real water samples (Y. Li et al., 2021).

A variety of precursors have been used to synthesize CDs, ranging from chemical molecules to waste materials. Recently, natural products and waste materials have attracted the interest of many researchers due to their environmental benefits. As an example, Zulfajri et al. (2019) reported the use of cranberry beans to develop CDs by a hydrothermal method; such CDs were applied as a fluorescent sensor to selectively detect Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. Similarly, Raveendran et al. (2019) used mint leaves to obtain highly luminescent CDs, which were able to detect Fe3+. Among the large variety of potential precursors, CA outstand for its popularity, mainly attributed to its low cost and biocompatibility (Ren et al., 2020). CA has been used as the main source of carbon to synthesize CDs through different techniques including microwave-assisted and hydrothermal methods; these CDs have been applied to detect a wide diversity of metal ions such as Fe3+ and Hg2+ (Pajewska-Szmyt et al., 2020; Pu et al., 2020; Shan et al., 2021). In addition, polymers and a vast diversity of small-scale molecules have been also applied for this purpose.

A relevant factor during CDs' synthesis is the selection of the carbon source since precursors' nature has a considerable effect on the properties of the resulting CDs (Chu et al., 2019; González-González et al., 2020). For instance, carbon sources play a vital role in the FL behavior of CDs because some of the chemical structures are originated from the carbon precursor (Lin et al., 2019a). Therefore, researchers have been constantly exploring different precursors to upgrade CDs’ properties.

The most recent advances on pristine CDs (unmodified CDs with post-treatments) for metal sensing applications are presented in Table 1 , demonstrating a wide diversity of precursors and synthesis methods. This extensive variety of carbon precursors and synthesis methods lead to CDs with different structures and properties such as differences in their particle size and quantum yield (QY) (Liu et al., 2019).

The selection of the precursor and synthesis method is fundamental to the surface functionalization of CDs. Consequently, the presence of surface functional groups has a significant effect on CDs' properties such as their stability, biocompatibility, and photoluminescence; therefore, characterization techniques like Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Raman are typically conducted on CDs’ studies for their identification. Ren et al. (2019) reported chemical functionalization as an effective method for the development of functional CDs. In addition, surface functionalization of photocatalysts or photosensitizers can hasten the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, avoid their recombination, and enhance charge migration by seizing the photogenerated electrons at a wide range of surface sites, which enables the control of the kinetics related to redox reactions (Sakdaronnarong et al., 2020). An example of the effect of surface functionalization was presented by Zheng et al. (2021), who through post-functionalization strategies constructed multifunctional CDs fluorescent probes for the independent detection of metal ions in water; an enhanced fluorescence response, a reduced limit of detection, and a broadened linear response interval was observed.

CDs prepared by wet chemical oxidation have shown higher functionalization, which has been attributed to a larger surface area with more developed textures on the activated carbons (Álvarez-Merino et al., 2005; Moreno-Castilla et al., 1997). This leads to more surface defects, which in turn narrows the energy levels and causes a bathochromic shift in CD's emission (Guo et al., 2018). The emission light of CDs can be regulated by the selection of different precursors. By the use of different biomasses, Lu et al. (2019) prepared nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with different functional groups on their surface such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino. Unlike CDs from chemical reagents, their biomass-derived CDs presented a larger particle size (Lu et al., 2019). Moreover, CDs derived from waste tires exhibited differences in their FL intensity and a bathochromic shift on their emission range due to an increased functionalization and oxidation degree (González-González et al., 2022b).

In this manner, functional groups on CDs' surfaces are extremely relevant for effectively tuning the intrinsic properties of CDs, modifying their chemical and electronic structure. For example, increased content of carbonyl and carboxyl groups in the surface of CDs have been associated with a higher emission efficiency (Guo et al., 2018). Similarly, amino, hydroxyl, and nitro groups have been identified on CDs’ surfaces (Yan et al., 2018). Since CDs are generally synthesized from organic precursors with oxidizing agents, CDs are regularly abundant in carboxyl groups with a remarkable water solubility and selectivity (Park et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). This strategy was applied by Chen et al. (2016), who synthesized CDs using CA and ethylenediamine followed by modification with 1,4,8,11 tetraazacyclotetradecane cyclam via amide coupling reaction to obtain Cyclam functionalized CDs to detect Cu2+ and sulfide (S2−) in an aqueous solution. On the other hand, CDs rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups derived from biomass resources exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior and were used as a selective sensor of Ag + which meets the requirements for environmental monitoring (Lu et al., 2019). Several reports have proved the potential of biomass-derived CDs for metal sensing; however, their functional groups are typically complex requiring further research to understand their mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms involved in the ability of CDs to detect metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: CDs can detect metal ions –or non-metal molecules– due to the CDs’ FL properties; remarkable effects on their FL are observed in the presence of specific metal ions. Various mechanisms have been reported as responsible for such FL changes when CDs are exposed to metal-contaminated samples, enabling their potential application as sensors for environmental monitoring. Typically, CDs exhibit a FL intensity which can be either enhanced or quenched when exposed to specific metal ions (Fig. 2 ). If the FL intensity of the synthesized CDs is enhanced when exposed to the analyte, the sensing mechanism is turn-on based. Frequently, turn-on CDs-based sensors are first combined with quenchers to achieve an “off-state”, followed by the interaction with the metal ion to reach the “on-state” (Tian et al., 2018). Some authors have suggested that turn-on FL sensors are preferable because of their high selectivity and wider detection range (Tian et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2012). The majority of the exposed targets can quench the FL of the CDs, which is physical or chemical cooperation amongst the CDs and the solvent, causing a decrease in the FL intensity (Lou et al., 2021). FL quenching can be achieved by different mechanisms such as static quenching and dynamic quenching.

FL quenching is an operation that contends with luminescence to reduce the lifespan of excited states of luminescent molecules. Nevertheless, static quenching is the relation between the quencher and the ground state molecules of CDs. Therefore, static quenching must fulfill three conditions: (i) CDs’ FL lifetime is not altered after integrating the quencher, (ii) the absorption spectrum of the CDs changes after adding the quencher, and (iii) the quenching constant declines when the temperature increases (Liu et al., 2017b).

On the other hand, dynamic quenching denotes that the excited state molecules of the CDs lose their excitation energy through the collision of the quencher molecules due to energy/charge transfer, being able to return to the ground state (Lou et al., 2021). Dynamic quenching is associated with the diffusion coefficient, which encourages the movement of molecules with the rise of temperature. Hence, dynamic quenching can satisfy the following conditions: (i) the presence of the quencher reduces the FL lifetime of CDs, (ii) the absorption spectrum of the carbon dots does not change after adding the quencher, and (iii) the quenching constant increases with increasing temperatures (Iqbal et al., 2016).

In addition to the optical properties, CDs have also exhibited photoinduced electron transfer (PET) property, which has been also associated with changes in CDs’ FL emission. PET is defined as an intramolecular process between the excited state of CDs and the analyte, in which a complex is formed between the electron donor and the electron acceptor (Yi et al., 2018). Mintz et al. (2018) investigated the interaction of CDs with several metal ions to provide insights on the quenching mechanisms of CDs by metals. They concluded that the quenching effect occurs because an electron from an amine functional group is transferred to the metal ions in proportion to the reduction potential of the metal (Mintz et al., 2018). However, further research efforts should be conducted in this direction to properly understand the PET properties of CDs and their influence on metal sensing applications. In Table 2 is summarized the most recent studies on carbon dots indicating the sensing mechanism for the detection of different metal ions. For most of the CDs-based FL sensors, metal ions are detected by a turn-off FL response, either via static or dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strategies for improving CDs’ performance in metal detection
ce:section.ce:para: CDs have shown great potentialities as sensors or probes to detect metal ions because of their tunable FL, water solubility, biocompatibility, and easy surface modification. However, pristine CDs typically report low values of FL quantum yield and easy contamination, which hinder their practical applications (Lin et al., 2019b; Liu et al., 2022). Therefore, the synthesis of CDs with high FL quantum yield through scalable processes has been recently the focus of research (Lin et al., 2019b). To enhance the FL properties of CDs and their performance for metal sensing applications, researchers have explored different strategies including element doping and surface passivation (Fig. 3 ).

Doped-CDs are receiving considerable research interest due to their outstanding advantages such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, photostability, and enhanced physicochemical properties (Ansari et al., 2021). Therefore, doping the structure of CDs either with heteroatoms or metallic elements has been extensively employed by several research groups to enhance their performances as sensors (Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021). The doped-CDs are defined as carbon-based nanoparticles with a particle size smaller than 10 nm in which atomic impurities are intentionally introduced causing modification in the chemical and electronic structure of CDs to improve their properties (Ansari et al., 2021). The elements most frequently employed for this purpose are nitrogen (N), phosphorous (P), sulfur (S), and boron (B) (Xia et al., 2019). In Table 3 , some of the most recent advances on metal sensing by doped-CDs are presented.

The element N outstands as the most frequently used heteroatom for the formation of doped-CDs due to the close similarity of the atomic radius between nitrogen and carbon, enabling a remarkable improvement in the photoluminescence properties of CDs (Ansari et al., 2021). In this manner, a vast number of studies have been conducted on the development of N-CDs aimed to detect metal ions (Atchudan et al., 2017; Y. Y. Ding et al., 2018; Gu et al., 2016; Kaur et al., 2019a). For instance, Ming et al. (2019) prepared N-CDs from thymidine and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen source. Such nitrogen-doped CDs were employed for the detection of Cr(VI) showing reliable results in the concentration range of 0.1–430
μ
M with a low limit of detection of 1.26 nM. This sensitive and selective FL probe showed good applicability in real water samples and the results obtained by N-CDs were consistent with the ones obtained by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), suggesting a great potential for environmental monitoring (Ming et al., 2019). Similar results were obtained by Atchudan et al. (2020), who prepared N-CDs from biowaste (dwarf banana peel) for the detection of Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution in the linear range of 5–25
μ
M and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.66
μ
M. The nitrogen-containing materials are extensive; however, some nitrogen precursors are repeatedly used such as ethylenediamine and urea.

Similar to N, the S atom is receiving increased research attention due to its effects; in particular, S introduces surface defects in CDs' structure which act as excitation energy traps causing an enhancement in their FL (Xu et al., 2015a). In addition, sulfur doped carbon dots (S-CDs) usually present a bathochromic shift in the maximum absorption wavelength caused by the lower electronegativity of S, absorbing light with lower energy to emit light with a longer wavelength (Lu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). S-CDs have been prepared from different sulfur-containing reactants such as sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3), methionine, and vitamin B1 (Ahmed et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2015a). Interestingly, the choice of the sulfur source influences the CDs’ properties. In this respect, Cunjin Wang et al. (2019) used sodium sulfide (Na2S) as a novel sulfur source; their S-CDs exhibited a larger FL intensity in comparison to undoped CDs, N-CDs, and S-CDs from other sulfur sources.

S-CDs have been successfully presented as effective heavy metal ion sensors. For example, Wu et al. (2018) prepared S-CDs from vitamin B1 and evaluated their performance on the detection of Fe3+ ions, reporting a LOD as low as 177 nM. Similarly, S-CDs prepared by Cunjin Wang et al. (2019) presented high sensitivity and selectivity to Hg2+ with a LOD of 0.222
μ
M. Therefore, the recent developments on S-CDs demonstrate its great potential for environmental monitoring of heavy metals.

The P element has been recently used as a dopant to alter the optical and electronic properties of CDs, because of its ability to act as an n-type donor and form surface defects in the structure of CDs (Ansari et al., 2021). To achieve proper P doping, phosphoric acid is usually reported as a P source (Ying Li et al., 2017; Omer and Hassan, 2017). Interestingly, there are some controversies on the effects caused by the P on CDs’ structures. For instance, some synthesized phosphorous doped carbon dots (P-CDs) have reported an excitation-independent behavior (Omer and Hassan, 2017), whereas some others exhibit excitation-dependent emission behavior (Molkenova and Atabaev, 2019; Sarkar et al., 2015). To shed light on this aspect, Kalaiyarasan et al. (2020) prepared P-CDs at different doping fractions of P by regulating the concentrations of trisodium citrate and phosphoric acid (P source). Their P-CDs exhibited an excitation-independent emission behavior. However, their results evidenced a direct proportionality between the P doping on CDs with the concentration of the P-containing source concluding that high concentrations of P precursors tend to increase the oxygen content instead of increasing P content, causing an excitation-dependent behavior (Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020).

In the literature exists different successful examples of the application of P-CDs to detect metal ions in water samples. For example, P-CDs prepared by Omer and Hassan (2017) were able to efficiently detect Al3+ and zinc (II) ion Zn2+. The P dopant had a significant effect on the QY, causing a notable enhancement from 12% to 62%, in comparison to the undoped CDs. This enhancement was attributed to the chelation of metal ions on the surface of P-CDs. Moreover, such P-CDs presented an enhanced FL in the presence of Zn2+ and Cd2+ (Omer and Hassan, 2017). Similarly, P-CDs prepared by Kalaiyarasan et al. (2020) were applied for the detection of Fe3+ showing good linearity in the range of 20–3000 nM with a LOD as low as 9.5 nM. The satisfactory results found on P-CDs tested in water samples demonstrate their potential to detect several metal ions.

Likewise, N, S, and P, B element can also be well incorporated into CDs acting as an effective p-type dopant modifying their optical and electronic properties (Ansari et al., 2021; Bourlinos et al., 2015). In comparison to undoped-CDs, boron doped carbon dots (B-CDs) have exhibited stronger FL and significantly higher nonlinear optical response (Bourlinos et al., 2015). Boric acid is a typical source of boron used during the synthesis of doped-CDs (Bourlinos et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2017a).

A representative example of their application as a metal ion sensor is presented by Wang et al. (2016); their B-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal synthesis using glucose and boric acid as the B source. They reported satisfactory results in the determination of Fe3+ in artificial water samples suggesting a potential application in environmental water samples and drinking water (Wang et al., 2016).

Recently, Sadhanala et al. (2021a, 2021b) synthesized B-CDs with high-quantum yield by a simple solvothermal method. In addition, they demonstrated B-CDs were highly selective and sensitive for the detection of magnesium ion (Mg2+) (Sadhanala et al., 2021a,b).

Likewise, mono-element doping (N-CDs, S-CDs, etc.), co-doped CDs with multiple heteroatoms is attracting research attention as an interesting strategy to enhance their properties and performances. This enhancement can be attributed to the individual benefits from each component and synergistic effects between different heteroatoms that form unique electronic structures (Ansari et al., 2021). As a result, higher QYs and longer photoluminescence emission wavelengths can be obtained (Li et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2015b). Furthermore, co-doping of CDs not only permits great improvements in photoluminescence and quantum yield efficiency but also delivers more active sites in CDs to widen their practical applications (Ding et al., 2014). Thus, their application in metal sensing has been explored by different authors as presented in Table 4 .

Frequently, sulfur and nitrogen are used as co-dopants to amplify the photoluminescence properties of CDs. For example, Zhang et al. (2018) reported the rapid synthesis of N,S-CDs via microwave-assisted using vitamin C and thiourea (TU) as precursors; such co-doped CDs were used for the detection of Fe3+, presenting a limit of detection as low as 4.2 nM.

Through a solvothermal technique, Qin et al. (2020) obtained N,S-CDs with remarkable high values of QY. Thus, their selectivity towards various metal ions was explored, presenting a strong FL quenching effect in the presence of Fe3+ and Ag+, with a limit of detection of 1.12
μ
M and 2.15
μ
M, respectively, suggesting that N,S-CDs can be applied in the monitoring of these metals in practical applications (Qin et al., 2020). Similar results have been presented by Cheng et al. (2019), who prepared N,S-CDs able to selectively detect Fe3+. However, their research outstands because of the use of cellulose-based biowaste as the co-doped CDs, which were synthesized by a facile combustion treatment in presence of urea and sulfuric acid (Cheng et al., 2019). Other authors have developed different methodologies to prepare N,S-CDs and have evaluated their performance in the detection of different metal ions providing low-cost, rapid, and sensitive fluorescent sensors for environmental applications (Pang et al., 2017; Wu and Tong, 2019).

N and P are also commonly found as co-dopants in CDs prepared for their application in metal sensing; they typically possess new and unique properties attributable to shared assets from each of the doped atoms. Nitrogen and phosphorous doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis from pyridoxal 5-phosphate and ethanediamine, which were investigated as fluorescent probes for Co2+ in the analysis of real river water samples; they presented good linearity in the range of 0–60
μ
M and a low LOD of 0.053
μ
M (Liao et al., 2018a). Similarly, Yang et al. (2021) prepared nitrogen and phosphorous doped graphene quantum dots (N,P-GQDs) through a hydrothermal methodology using CA and phosphonitrilic chloride trimer as precursors. Their co-doped nanomaterial exhibited excellent optical stability, high QY good solubility, and high-performance as Fe3+ sensor with an ultra-sensitive LOD of 146 nM (Yang et al., 2021).

Doping CDs by B and N is considered an excellent option to adjust their fluorescent properties, mainly because: (i) the nearest neighbors of the C in the periodic table are N and B, (ii) N and B have a similar atomic radius to C, and (iii) the substitution of C by N or B leads to changes in the local chemical reactivity that results in improvements of the optical performance (Jana et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2020). Nitrogen and boron doped carbon dots (N,B-CDs) from ammonium citrate and bis(pinacolato) diboron via hydrothermal method were synthesized. In comparison to the undoped CDs, N,B-CDs showed enhanced FL properties. For instance, the FL quantum yield was calculated as high as 59%. Their co-doped CDs were efficiently used as a FL probe to detect Cr(VI) in the linear range of 0.3–500
μ
M, and a LOD of 0.24
μ
M (Wang et al., 2020).

On the other hand, doping with more than two heteroatoms can lead to CDs with narrow emission bands, possibly due to the generation of a more homogeneous and reduced bandgap (Ansari et al., 2021). As an example, Wang et al. (2014) prepared CDs doped with N, P, and S for the detection of Hg2+. They concluded that the obtained nitrogen, phosphorus and sulfur-doped carbon dots (N,P,S-CDs) exhibited good luminescence stability, high solubility, excitation-dependent emission behavior, and good selectivity for Hg2+ in a wide linear range (1–70
μ
M) in aqueous solution (Wang et al., 2014). Although the exact mechanisms of co-doped CDs with multiple heteroatoms remain unclear, it is reasonable to believe that the co-doping technique can enhance the performance of CDs in chemical sensing.

Generally, most of the as-synthesized CDs (pristine CDs) exhibit low values of FL quantum yield. To improve CDs’ FL properties, some researchers have performed addition passivation processes, typically with organic molecules or polymers (Table 5 ). As a result of the surface passivation, enhanced photoluminescence is caused attributed to surface energy traps that become emissive (Pan et al., 2017). In this respect, Pacquiao et al. (2018) prepared CDs from enokitake mushroom via the hydrothermal method; unmodified and passivated CDs were employed for the detection of Cr6+. The unmodified-CDs presented a QY of 11%; however, after passivation with tetraethylenepentamine, the QY increased to 39%. They concluded that natural materials such as enokitake mushroom can be used to produce high-value CDs with good applicability to detect Cr6+ with a limit of detection as low as 0.73
μ
M (Pacquiao et al., 2018).

The passivation of CDs can be achieved by three different approaches (Fig. 4 ): (i) when the precursor act both as carbon source and passivating agent, as reported by Tan et al. (2016) who used 1,2-Hexadecanediol as the carbon precursor and the surface passivation agent for the synthesis of highly bright oil-soluble CDs; (ii) when the passivating agent is added combined with the carbon precursor during the CDs’ synthesis, as in the research presented by Pappalardo et al. (2020) who synthesized CDs through microwave-assisted method using CA, d-galactose, and Poly(dopamine); and (iii) when the passivation process is performed post-synthesis via covalent tethering of a molecule to the CDs, as Dubois et al. (2007) described on their research.

Researchers have employed a wide diversity of passivating agents; among them, polyethylene glycol (PEG) outstands for its popularity for the passivation of CDs. PEG, as a synthetic biocompatible polymer with a repeating subunit of ethylene glycol, possesses superior properties such as water solubility and nontoxicity providing better characteristics to CDs (Peng et al., 2020). PEG has been used as both a carbon source and passivating agent for the synthesis of CDs by simple hydrothermal methods (Fan et al., 2014). The use of ethylenediamine (EDA) as a passivating agent during the synthesis of CDs has also reported excellent results. EDA can act as an N-doping precursor and surface passivating agent, which leads to the formation of CDs with higher QYs (Zheng et al., 2015). To avoid arduous and time-consuming passivating post-treatments, Niu et al. (2015) proposed a simultaneous hydrothermal process of CDs’ formation and passivation with EDA, producing CDs with a QY as high as 46%. This high value was attributed to the interplay between the holes trapped and the surface passivation of CDS (Niu et al., 2015).

Metal detection is among the exciting applications of passivated-CDs. In this regard, Li et al. (2021) prepared CDs by a simple and scalable pyrolysis method using CA and EDA. Their CDs presented a high QY (65.5%), an excitation-independent emission behavior, and high selectivity on the detection of Fe3+ in the range of 0–400
μ
M with a limit of detection as low as 0,703
μ
M. Similarly, Wang et al. (2021) produced passivated-CDs via the hydrothermal method and using hyperbranched polyethylenimine as the passivating agent. Their passivated-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity and anti-interference capacity on the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution, showing a good linear response concentration range (2–360
μ
M) with a low limit of detection (0.47
μ
M) (Wang et al., 2021a). In addition, such fluorescent probes have been successfully tested in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021).

The great potential of CDs in environmental monitoring is mainly attributed to their fascinating characteristics like their unique physicochemical properties, affordability, high biocompatibility, and eco-friendliness. However, to achieve practical applicability in real wastewater samples, some challenges of current concern should be addressed. For instance, the extensive variety of raw materials or precursors, and the many possible synthetic approaches, lead to uncertainty or difficulties in the understanding of CDs’ properties. In addition, the effects of CDs released into the environment on biological populations and ecosystems have been uncommonly researched. Therefore, the biological toxicity and ecological effects of CDs should be considered for further research.

In the literature is reported an assortment of synthesis techniques for producing CDs with variable properties. Researchers have been exploring innovative, green, and straightforward synthetic strategies to fulfill the requirements of affordable and eco-friendly CDs for multiple applications. In this regard, further efforts should be conducted on the use of natural biomass/waste materials as the carbon source and the understanding of their consequent tuned properties. Moreover, improvements on the synthesis treatments should be focused on the reduction of some requirements like high temperatures, energy consumption, and use of solvents, while considering ease of operation, scalability, reaction time, and environmental impact.

Great advancements have been made in the development of CDs-based sensory platforms; however, there are still some challenges to overcome. Wastewater is typically a complex mixture of pollutants; thus, CDs-based sensors with an independent detection of multiple metal ions and anti-interference capacities are highly desired. Also, future studies should consider the effect of parameters such as co-existing molecules and different pH values. Indeed, the performance of CDs' in real and complex water systems should be examined in detail. Similarly, further efforts should be conducted to shed light on the understanding of the mechanisms involved in the selectivity of the metal ions. Strategies to improve recoveries and reusability of CDs fluorescent probes should be also explored; in this regard, CDs’ immobilization into solid substrates could represent an alternative to develop commercial tests.

In this review, the potentialities of CDs for metal sensing applications have been carefully revised. The most recent advances on CDs as metal sensors were discussed in terms of the effects caused by the selection of precursors and synthesis methods on CDs' properties, the mechanisms involved in CDs’ ability to detect metal ions, and the strategies to enhance their performance. In this manner, heteroatom-doping and surface passivation processes represent excellent alternatives to enhance the performance of CDs, reaching superior FL quantum yields and high sensitivity for diverse metal ions. In light of the great demand for affordable, accurate, and sustainable fluorescent probes for environmental monitoring, the current challenges of CDs to achieve research progress and commercial applications were also described. In this sense, is of great relevance to direct research efforts towards: (i) the optimization of the response in real and complex water samples; (ii) the understanding of the mechanisms involved in the selectivity of metal ions; (iii) the development of strategies to improve recoveries and reusability of CDs fluorescent probes; and (iv) the evaluation of the ecological effects caused by the application of CDs.
